steps.action: The lakalaka (walking briskly) is a Tongan group with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, accent beat, grounded.
steps.text: The lakalaka (walking briskly) is a Tongan group with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, accent beat, grounded.

steps.action: It is considered as the national dance of Tonga
steps.text: It is considered as the national dance of Tonga and part of the intangible human heritage.

steps.action: It is the ideal dance at formal occasions, like
steps.text: It is the ideal dance at formal occasions, like the birthday of the king or the opening of a church.

steps.action: In any case, none of these laws specified exactl
steps.text: In any case, none of these laws specified exactly what was meant by 'heathen dance'.

steps.action: There are reports of some huge dance festivals d
steps.text: There are reports of some huge dance festivals during these times, and no reports of any arrests.

steps.action: Admittedly, the pre-missionary pōmeʻe (night dan
steps.text: Admittedly, the pre-missionary pōmeʻe (night dances), after which couples disappeared into the bushes, did not occur anymore.

steps.action: Considering that the dance had never stopped bei
steps.text: Considering that the dance had never stopped being practiced, it would probably be better to say that he revitalised it.

steps.action: Tuku used missionary-approved nursery rhymes and
steps.text: Tuku used missionary-approved nursery rhymes and added some simple gestures and steps.

steps.action: Thus, a new dance was born, but still retained m
steps.text: Thus, a new dance was born, but still retained many of its original characteristics, and was approved by the missionaries.

steps.action: Often when a celebration is coming up, a punake
steps.text: Often when a celebration is coming up, a punake (poet) will write the lyrics to the occasion, assign music to the stanzas from a pool of typical tunes and then choreograph the haka (dance movements).

steps.action: The dance movements of men and women are differe
steps.text: The dance movements of men and women are different.

steps.action: Most of the time the women make small steps to t
steps.text: Most of the time the women make small steps to the left and right only, and their arm movements are small and fluid.

steps.action: In addition to small steps, they may at times tu
steps.text: In addition to small steps, they may at times turn around, sit down or even lie down.

steps.action: In any case, how different their haka on first g
steps.text: In any case, how different their haka on first glance may be, both men and women interpret the words of the songs, but in a symbolic, allusive way as so typical for Tongan dance.

steps.action: The dancers are standing in one or more rows, de
steps.text: The dancers are standing in one or more rows, depending on the number of participants.

steps.action: Behind them the chorus; the lakalaka is a sung d
steps.text: Behind them the chorus; the lakalaka is a sung dance, there is none or very little instrumental accompaniment.

steps.action: The dance normally starts with the singing of th
steps.text: The dance normally starts with the singing of the first stanza by both dancers and chorus, which is a deference to the god, the king and the chiefs of the country.

steps.action: On the next stanza dancing starts.
steps.text: On the next stanza dancing starts.

steps.action: First calmly and subdued, but toward the end bec
steps.text: First calmly and subdued, but toward the end becoming wilder and wilder, while the tempo of the beat goes up as well.

steps.action: The third positions are filled with the best dan
steps.text: The third positions are filled with the best dancer of the male and female group respectively, the mālie taha.

steps.action: It is for a dance group a high honour if a princ
steps.text: It is for a dance group a high honour if a prince or princess or noble will participate as vāhenga.

steps.action: Because all Tongan dances, especially formal one
steps.text: Because all Tongan dances, especially formal ones like the lakalaka are in fact a deference to the chiefs.

steps.action: Therefore, people higher in rank than the guest
steps.text: Therefore, people higher in rank than the guest of honour at a particular celebration cannot dance.

steps.action: A king or queen can never dance.
steps.text: A king or queen can never dance.
